serving.description: 1 slice (approximately 1/8 of a standard 30 cm pizza)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetable pizza is one slice weighing approximately 100 g, representing roughly one-eighth of a standard 30 cm round pizza.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Pizza — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Vegetable Pizza (cr_id G00010174). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry; the value is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cheese pizza (USDA #21299) adjusted proportionally for a lower-cheese vegetable variant.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetable pizza in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 80 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'Pizza, plain, white flour base' (GI ~80, University of Sydney GI Database). The high refined-flour dough content and high sugar (15.75 g/100g from tomato sauce) support a high GI classification. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (80 × 32.38) / 100 = 25.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (15.75 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) triggers the solid-food sugar gate (>10 g sugar, fibre <2 g), promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure despite the relatively low sodium (130 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit vegetable pizza in this formulation; request a wholemeal-crust version with reduced tomato sauce to lower sugar below 10 g/100g and increase fibre above 2 g/100g before reconsidering suitability.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 15.75 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.7 g/100g triggers the diabetes hard gate (sugar >15 g/100g, fibre <2 g); the high-GI refined dough (GI ~80) and high glycemic load (~25.9 per 100 g) create a rapid postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this pizza formulation; if consumed, limit to a very small portion (≤50 g), pair with a high-fibre side salad, and monitor blood glucose closely — a wholemeal or cauliflower-crust alternative with reduced sauce is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (7.53 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat (~1.8 g/100g) and high sugar (15.75 g/100g) contribute to dyslipidaemia risk; sodium at 130 mg/100g is within acceptable limits but cumulative intake across a full serving warrants monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one slice (≈100 g), choose a wholemeal crust to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio, and request reduced cheese and sauce to lower saturated fat and sugar content in line with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 15.75 g/100g exceeds the 15 g refined-sugar RA gate, promoting pro-inflammatory mediators; tomato sauce and capsicum are nightshade-family foods that may aggravate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this pizza formulation due to high sugar and nightshade content; a lower-sugar preparation with wholemeal crust, reduced tomato sauce, and increased mushroom and spinach toppings may be tolerated with individual monitoring.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Pizza is a baked flatbread-based dish consisting of a refined wheat-flour pizza dough base spread with tomato sauce and topped with assorted vegetables including capsicum (bell pepper) and onions, with minimal cheese. As sold and consumed in Singapore, this pizza variant is classified under grains and staples due to its dominant dough component. Per 100 g, it provides 204 kcal, 32.4 g carbohydrate, 7.5 g fat, 1.8 g protein, and a notably high sugar content of 15.75 g — largely attributable to concentrated tomato sauce — alongside only 1.7 g dietary fibre. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio gives this food a high glycemic index (estimated ~80) and a high glycemic load (~25.9 per 100 g serving), making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. Sodium is relatively low at 130 mg/100g. The pizza is vegetarian and typically halal-certified in Singapore food service contexts. Clinically, the high sugar content with low fibre disqualifies this food for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis under standard dietary gate criteria. The capsicum and tomato sauce are nightshade-family foods relevant to RA-sensitive patients. A wholemeal or cauliflower crust with reduced tomato sauce would substantially improve the nutritional profile. The dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item and is widely available across Singapore food courts, cafes, and pizza chains.
common_user_queries: Is veggie pizza suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in a slice of vegetable pizza?
Is vegetable pizza high in sugar?
What is the GI of vegetable pizza?
Is vegetable pizza good for weight loss?
Can I eat vegetable pizza if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010174) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous entry and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain white-flour pizza base, which is the closest defensible approved-source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cheese pizza (USDA #21299), adjusted downward to reflect the lower-cheese vegetable variant; this value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific vegetable pizza preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry G00010174 as of 2026-04-16. 3. The input record incorrectly assigned 'Middle Eastern' as the primary cuisine; this has been corrected to 'Western / International' as pizza is an Italian-origin dish adapted internationally. 4. GI of 80 is an analogous estimate based on 'Pizza, plain, white flour base' from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct vegetable pizza GI entry was found in the approved GI source. 5. Health suitability assessments from the input record have been preserved and reformatted into the health_context structure with the addition of cardiovascular disease as a mandatory condition.
